title: Chat System
prompt: Design a real-time chat system like Slack or WhatsApp. Users should be able to send messages in 1:1 conversations and group chats, see online status, and receive messages in real-time.

nonFunctional: Message ordering guarantees within a conversation

keyQuestions: 1:1 only or group chat too? Max group size?
How long do we store messages?

keyCalculations: Storage: 100B messages * 200 bytes = 20TB/year for text only
Fan-out for group messages: avg group 50 members => 2.5M deliveries/sec
Connection servers: 50M connections / 100K per server = 500 servers

expectedEntities: messages: id, conversation_id, sender_id, content, type, created_at, sequence_num

expectedComponents: Message Store (database + blob storage for media)
Session Registry (which server has which user's connection)

suggestedTopics: WebSocket connection management and session routing
Fan-out strategy for group messages (push vs pull)
Presence system: heartbeat-based with gossip protocol
Offline message delivery and push notification pipeline
Database partitioning: by conversation_id for locality

anchors.1: Jumped to design without understanding chat vs messaging distinction
anchors.2: Asked about basic features but missed ordering or offline delivery
anchors.3: Covered 1:1 + group, real-time delivery, offline support, and scale
anchors.4: Proactively identified fan-out challenges, ordering complexities, and presence at scale

anchors.1: HTTP polling with single database, no real-time consideration
anchors.2: WebSocket but no connection routing, single-server assumption
anchors.3: Multi-server WebSocket with session registry, message queue, and presence
anchors.4: Clean layered architecture with failure isolation, justified each component choice

anchors.1: No concrete implementation for any component
anchors.2: Surface-level discussion of WebSocket or database without trade-offs
anchors.3: Detailed design of 2+ components with concrete approaches
anchors.4: Deep analysis of ordering guarantees, fan-out trade-offs, presence protocol with failure handling

checklist: Push vs pull for group delivery
Consistency vs availability for message ordering
WebSocket vs long polling vs SSE
SQL vs NoSQL for message storage

anchors.3: Analyzed 2-3 trade-offs with clear reasoning for choices
anchors.4: Comprehensive trade-off analysis across real-time, storage, and delivery dimensions

commonMistakes: Using HTTP polling instead of persistent connections for real-time
Not addressing how to route messages when users connect to different servers
Ignoring message ordering in distributed system
Fan-out on write for large groups (should use fan-out on read for 500+ member groups)
Not separating presence from messaging (presence generates much higher traffic)
Storing messages in a single unpartitioned database
